name: Andromeda
mythology: Princess of Aethiopia, daughter of Cepheus and Cassiopeia, chained to a rock to be devoured by Cetus, saved by Perseus.

name: Antlia
mythology: Created by Nicolas-Louis de Lacaille in 1752 to honor the invention of the air pump by Denis Papin.

name: Apus
mythology: Represents the exotic Bird of Paradise, introduced by Petrus Plancius in the late 16th century.

name: Aquarius
mythology: Associated with Ganymede, the cupbearer of the Greek gods, who poured water from a cup to feed the Earth.

name: Aquila
mythology: The eagle that carried Zeus's thunderbolts and abducted the young Ganymede to Mount Olympus.

name: Ara
mythology: The altar where the gods of Olympus swore allegiance before defeating the Titans in Greek mythology.

name: Aries
mythology: The golden ram that saved Phrixus and Helle, whose golden fleece became the target of Jason and the Argonauts.

name: Auriga
mythology: Associated with Erichthonius, the lame king of Athens who invented the four-horse chariot to get around.

name: Bootes
mythology: The celestial herdsman who chases Ursa Major around the North Pole, often associated with the inventor of the plow.

name: Caelum
mythology: Introduced by Lacaille in 1752, representing the sculptor's drafting chisel.

name: Camelopardalis
mythology: Created by Petrus Plancius in 1613 to represent the camel that Rebekah rode to marry Isaac in the Bible.

name: Cancer
mythology: The giant crab sent by Hera to distract Heracles while he fought the Lernaean Hydra. Strangled by Heracles.

name: Canes Venatici
mythology: The hunting dogs of Bootes, named Asterion and Chara, placed in the sky by Johannes Hevelius in 1687.

name: Canis Major
mythology: Representing Laelaps, the swiftest dog in the world, who was destined to catch whatever he chased.

name: Canis Minor
mythology: The smaller companion dog of Orion, containing the bright star Procyon, which rises just before Canis Major.

name: Capricornus
mythology: Associated with Pan, who jumped into the Nile to escape Typhon, transforming his lower half into a fish.

name: Carina
mythology: The keel of the Argo Navis, the legendary ship of Jason and the Argonauts who sought the Golden Fleece.

name: Cassiopeia
mythology: Vain queen of Aethiopia who claimed her beauty surpassed the sea nymphs. Sentenced to circle the pole on her throne.

name: Centaurus
mythology: Represents Chiron, the wise centaur who mentored Greek heroes like Jason, Heracles, and Achilles.

name: Cepheus
mythology: Mythical King of Aethiopia, husband of Cassiopeia, father of Andromeda, placed in the stars near the North Pole.

name: Cetus
mythology: The sea monster sent by Poseidon to ravage Aethiopia, slain by Perseus using the petrifying head of Medusa.

name: Chamaeleon
mythology: Introduced by Dutch navigators in the late 16th century, representing the color-changing lizard of the tropics.

name: Circinus
mythology: Created by Lacaille in 1752, representing the pair of compasses used by draughtsmen and navigators.

name: Columba
mythology: The dove sent out from Noah's Ark to search for dry land, or the dove Jason sent to guide the Argo through the Symplegades.

name: Coma Berenices
mythology: The amber hair of Queen Berenice of Egypt, sacrificed to Aphrodite to ensure her husband returned safely from war.

name: Corona Australis
mythology: Represents the wreath of laurel leaves worn by the centaur Chiron, or a crown placed by Dionysus to honor his mother Semele.

name: Corona Borealis
mythology: The crown of Ariadne, daughter of King Minos, which Zeus placed in the heavens after her wedding to Dionysus.

name: Corvus
mythology: The sacred white crow of Apollo that was turned black as a punishment for lying about a maiden's infidelity.

name: Crater
mythology: The golden mixing cup belonging to Apollo, which was guarded by the crow Corvus in a Greek legend.

name: Crux
mythology: The famous Southern Cross, historically used by sailors in the Southern Hemisphere to find South.

name: Cygnus
mythology: Zeus transformed into a swan to visit Leda. Also associated with Cycnus, who grieved for Phaethon.

name: Delphinus
mythology: The dolphin that helped Poseidon persuade the sea nymph Amphitrite to marry him. Placed in the stars in gratitude.

name: Dorado
mythology: Represents the mahi-mahi or swordfish, created by Dutch navigators in the late 16th century.

name: Draco
mythology: Ladon, the hundred-headed dragon that guarded the golden apples in the Garden of the Hesperides, slain by Heracles.

name: Equuleus
mythology: Associated with Celeris, the brother of Pegasus, given to Castor by Hermes in Greek mythology.

name: Eridanus
mythology: The celestial river where Phaethon fell after losing control of Helios's sun chariot, burning the Earth.

name: Fornax
mythology: Created by Lacaille in 1752, representing the chemical furnace used in scientific experiments.

name: Gemini
mythology: Castor and Pollux, twin sons of Leda, who shared immortality so they could remain together forever after Castor died.

name: Grus
mythology: Introduced by Plancius in 1598, representing the crane bird, symbol of vigilance in ancient Egypt.

name: Hercules
mythology: The greatest Greek hero, son of Zeus, who accomplished the Twelve Labors to atone for his madness.

name: Horologium
mythology: Named by Lacaille to honor Christiaan Huygens, the inventor of the pendulum clock in the 17th century.

name: Hydra
mythology: The multi-headed Lernaean Hydra slain by Heracles as his second labor, whose blood was deadly poison.

name: Hydrus
mythology: Created by Plancius to represent the water snakes encountered by explorers in southern waters.

name: Indus
mythology: Represents the indigenous people of the Americas or Asia, created by late 16th-century navigators.

name: Lacerta
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1687, representing a small lizard on the boundary of Cygnus and Andromeda.

name: Leo
mythology: The Nemean Lion defeated by Heracles as his first labor, whose impervious hide Heracles wore as armor.

name: Leo Minor
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1687 to fill the blank space between Ursa Major and the zodiacal Leo.

name: Lepus
mythology: The hare that Orion was said to be hunting, placed below Orion's feet to signify the eternal chase.

name: Libra
mythology: The scales of Astraea, goddess of justice, historically representing the equality of day and night at the equinox.

name: Lupus
mythology: Historically a generic beast speared by the Centaur, later identified as a wolf sacrificing to the gods.

name: Lynx
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1687; he noted one would need the eyes of a lynx to see this faint constellation.

name: Lyra
mythology: The magical lyre of Orpheus, whose music could charm stones, trees, and make Hades weep in the underworld.

name: Mensa
mythology: Named by Lacaille after Table Mountain in South Africa, where he conducted his southern hemisphere observations.

name: Microscopium
mythology: Named by Lacaille in 1752 to honor the invention of the compound microscope.

name: Monoceros
mythology: Introduced by Plancius in 1612 to represent the mythical unicorn mentioned in the Bible.

name: Musca
mythology: Created by Plancius in 1598, originally named Apis (The Bee) but later renamed Musca (The Fly) to avoid confusion.

name: Norma
mythology: Named by Lacaille in 1752, representing the level and square used by shipwrights and carpenters.

name: Octans
mythology: Named by Lacaille to honor the invention of the octant, precursor to the modern sextant. Contains the South Pole.

name: Ophiuchus
mythology: Asclepius, the ancient Greek physician who could raise the dead, killed by Zeus with a thunderbolt to prevent immortality.

name: Orion
mythology: Giant huntsman placed by Zeus in the stars. He stands with his club raised against Taurus the Bull.

name: Pavo
mythology: The sacred bird of Hera, placed in the stars by late 16th-century navigators to represent a peacock.

name: Pegasus
mythology: The winged horse that sprung from Medusa's neck when Perseus cut it off, ridden by Bellerophon to fight the Chimera.

name: Perseus
mythology: Greek hero who slew Medusa and saved Andromeda from the sea monster Cetus, wielding the sword of Hermes.

name: Phoenix
mythology: Introduced by Plancius in 1598, representing the mythical bird that dies in flames and rises from its own ashes.

name: Pictor
mythology: Named by Lacaille to represent the artist's easel, near the bright star Canopus.

name: Pisces
mythology: Aphrodite and Eros transformed into fish and tied themselves together with a cord to escape the monster Typhon.

name: Piscis Austrinus
mythology: The Great Fish that drank the water poured out by the water bearer Aquarius, containing the bright star Fomalhaut.

name: Puppis
mythology: The stern of the mythological Argo Navis, the colossal ship of Jason and the Argonauts.

name: Pyxis
mythology: The magnetic compass of the Argo Navis, created by Lacaille in 1752, replacing a previous constellation.

name: Reticulum
mythology: Named by Lacaille in 1752 to represent the reticle grid in his observatory telescope.

name: Sagitta
mythology: The arrow shot by Heracles to kill the eagle that tortured Prometheus, or the arrow of Cupid.

name: Sagittarius
mythology: Represents the centaur archer Crotus, who was a skilled hunter and friend of the Muses on Mount Helicon.

name: Scorpius
mythology: The giant scorpion sent by Gaia to kill the hunter Orion. Antares represents its glowing red heart.

name: Sculptor
mythology: Created by Lacaille in 1752, representing the workshop of a sculptor with a bust and tools.

name: Scutum
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1684 to honor King John III Sobieski of Poland, who defended Europe at Vienna.

name: Serpens
mythology: The giant serpent held by Ophiuchus, split into Serpens Caput (Head) and Serpens Cauda (Tail).

name: Sextans
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1687 to commemorate the astronomical sextant he used for stellar mapping.

name: Taurus
mythology: Zeus transformed into a white bull to carry away Europa. Contains the Pleiades cluster at the shoulder.

name: Telescopium
mythology: Created by Lacaille in 1752 to honor the telescope and the work of Hans Lippershey and Galileo.

name: Triangulum
mythology: Represents the island of Sicily, placed in the stars by Hermes at Ceres' request, or the Delta symbol.

name: Triangulum Australe
mythology: Created by Plancius in 1598, representing a perfect triangle of three bright southern stars.

name: Tucana
mythology: Represents the toucan bird of South America, created by Plancius in the late 16th century.

name: Ursa Major
mythology: The nymph Callisto who was transformed into a bear by Hera, then placed in the stars by Zeus to save her life.

name: Ursa Minor
mythology: Arcas, son of Callisto, transformed into a bear to join his mother. Contains the North Star, Polaris.

name: Vela
mythology: The sails of the mythological ship Argo Navis, which was split into three modern constellations by Lacaille.

name: Virgo
mythology: Astraea, goddess of justice, who abandoned humanity in the Bronze Age due to wickedness, taking her scales with her.

name: Volans
mythology: Created by late 16th-century navigators, representing a flying fish chased by Dorado (the swordfish).

name: Vulpecula
mythology: Introduced by Hevelius in 1687 as a fox carrying a goose (Anser) in its jaws to the gods.
